title: Nasopharyngeal Airway -- Equipment
content: Generally, the NPA size is around the same size as an ET tube or 0.5 mm larger. [10] NPA size selection is generally based on the length, not the internal diameter, so the device's tip sits just above the epiglottis. Studies found that patient height was a better predictor of proper NPA size than the nares-to-earlobe and nares-to-mandible techniques. [11] Some experts propose that if NPAs of different lengths exist for a particular diameter—like what some manufacturers produce—the longest one should be used, preferably with an adjustable flange. [12]

These give a more comfortable postoperative period for the patient and use less anesthetic.

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Teeth -- Surgical Considerations
content: When making a treatment plan for a questionable tooth, many factors come into play to decide whether the tooth should be treated and maintained, or extracted and replaced by an implant. From a periodontal standpoint, the prognosis of a tooth is determined by its amount of attachment loss and probing pocket depth. The progressive loss of periodontal attachment and increase in probe pocket depth are predictive of additional disease activity increases the likelihood of needing to be extracted. If the bone loss is too great with the periodontal loss, an implant would only be a possible replacement with successful bone volume augmentation. From a tooth structural standpoint, the prognosis of a tooth is determined by its amount of coronal structure remaining. A prosthetic crown can be placed on the tooth if there is sufficient structure remaining to retain the crown. Crown lengthening and extrusion can increase the amount of tooth structure for sufficient retention of a dental crown when appropriate. [17]

title: Esophageal Diverticula -- Treatment Planning
content: Endoscopic myotomy (Dohlman Procedure) demands neck extension; therefore, it would not be an appropriate choice for patients with cervical stenosis who would not tolerate neck extension. The definitive management of symptomatic Zenker diverticulum is the surgical approach. The decision to use either an open or endoscopic transoral approach (rigid endoscope or a flexible endoscope) is made by clinicians based on several factors. The most important factors to be considered are 1. the ability to visualize the ZD and septum endoscopically, 2. patient's body habitus, 3. the ZD pouch feature in relation to the posterior wall, and 4. local expertise. Accordingly, patients with short necks decreased hyomental distance, and/or those patients with obesity are most often managed with an open approach. However, diverticula with less than 2 cm length are usually managed with endoscopic methods.

title: Double J Placement Methods Comparative Analysis -- Technique or Treatment -- Load the Stent
content: Advance the guidewire into the stent. Place one hand on the distal end of the plastic outer sheath and pinch the stent and plastic sheath together. This extra stability facilitates the passage of the guidewire through the stent as otherwise, the guidewire may push the stent out the back of the plastic sheath. Once the guidewire is through the proximal coiled end of the stent, slide the sheath outwards to straighten out the distal coil and push the guide wire all the way through. Remove the plastic outer sheath once the guidewire has passed through the entire stent and is visible at the distal end.  Do not remove the thread yet.

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Asterion -- Clinical Significance
content: A study on 100 dried skulls was conducted using a 2 mm drill bit perpendicular to the skull at the asterion. The distance of the asterion from the zygomatic process, the suprameatal crest, and the mastoid tip were studied. It was found to be located superficial to transverse sigmoid sinus junction in 87%, inferior in 11%, and superior in 2% of the skulls. The location and anatomical relations of the asterion might prove beneficial in microvascular trigeminal root decompression and petrosal approaches. [7] The surface and intracranial location of the asterion and anatomical relation with the dural venous sinuses have been studied to help neurosurgeons avoid fatal complications while using the burr hole technique in retrosigmoid approaches. [8] [9]

title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2025
content: Cervical cerclage suture present, polyp of cervix affecting pregnancy, previous operation to cervix affecting pregnancy, and rigid cervix uteri affecting pregnancy are all findings that have the cervix uteri as their anatomic site. These entities represent structural or pathological conditions of the cervix that can influence pregnancy outcomes. A cervical cerclage suture is placed to reinforce a weakened cervix and prevent premature dilation, while a cervical polyp can interfere with implantation or cause bleeding during gestation. Prior cervical surgery may alter cervical integrity and increase the risk of cervical insufficiency, and a rigid cervix can impede normal cervical remodeling required for labor. Recognizing that each of these findings is located in the cervix uteri helps clinicians assess cervical health, anticipate potential obstetric complications, and tailor management strategies to support a safe pregnancy.

title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1895
content: Removal of cerclage from cervix is a medical intervention that is directly related to the cervical cerclage procedure (إجراء تطويق عنق الرحم). This step involves extracting the sutures that were placed around the cervix to reinforce cervical competence and prevent premature dilation, and it represents a specific phase within the overall management of cervical insufficiency. Recognizing the connection between the removal of the cerclage and the broader cerclage procedure is essential for appropriate patient care and timing of intervention.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2347
content: In obstetric practice, the overarching topic “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery” includes the specific condition “Malposition; malpresentation,” which is classified as a subcategory (has parent) within these care indications. This relationship highlights that malposition and malpresentation are considered part of the broader set of indications guiding clinical management during pregnancy, labor, and delivery.
